AI.motivation: To explore and highlight the vulnerabilities in verifier-driven self-improvement setups for production visual-language models, particularly how a task-specific failure of verifiers could silently cause regression.
AI.method: The study evaluates verifier performance on open-source verifier models across different tasks (MathVista, MMMU, BLINK), analyzes their effect on model progression, and provides a theoretical explaination via a variance theorem for certain failure modes.
AI.result: Findings reveal that verifier quality varies by task, with high-performing verifiers on one task regressing model performance on others. Accuracy drops by 3.4 to 10.9 percentage points were observed, showcasing task-specific mismatch and confidence-inverted damage within the training system.
AI.conclusion: Verifier-driven loops should incorporate task-specific measures of rubric accuracy rather than relying on general performance indicators to prevent regression and optimize resource allocation.

title: From Self-Supervised Speech Models to Mixture-of-Experts for Robust Anti-Spoofing
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, accepted at Odyssey 2026 (The Speaker and Language Recognition Workshop)
summary: Recent advances in speech generation have significantly improved the naturalness of synthetic speech, making spoofing detection increasingly challenging. A key limitation of current anti-spoofing systems is their limited robustness to unseen synthesis methods. In this work, we transform a self-supervised speech representation model into a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to improve generalization. Feed-forward blocks in selected encoder layers are replaced by multiple expert networks controlled by a layer-wise gating mechanism, allowing experts to capture complementary acoustic patterns while preserving the representations learned during self-supervised pretraining. We further analyze the architectural choices affecting the performance of this MoE conversion and investigate the activation behavior of the experts. The proposed approach is evaluated on 14 spoofing datasets and reduces the macro EER from 5.46% to 4.81%, corresponding to 11.9% relative improvement over the baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture to improve robustness in spoofing detection systems, achieving notable improvements in generalization across unseen synthesis methods.
AI.motivation: Current anti-spoofing systems struggle with robustness when faced with unseen synthesis methods, creating the need for an approach that enhances generalization.
AI.method: The approach modifies a self-supervised speech representation model by integrating a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, with expert networks controlled by a layer-wise gating mechanism to capture complementary acoustic patterns.
AI.result: The proposed method was tested on 14 spoofing datasets, achieving a reduction in macro Equal Error Rate (EER) from 5.46% to 4.81%, representing an 11.9% relative improvement compared to the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Transforming self-supervised models into MoE architectures improves the robustness and generalization of anti-spoofing systems, offering meaningful advancements in tackling challenges posed by unseen synthesis methods.

title: Listening with Attention: Entropy-Guided Explainability for Transformer-Based Audio Models
comment: 17 pages, 3 figures, and 9 tables. Accepted in Interspeech 2026 conference
summary: Transformer-based automatic speech recognition (ASR) models such as Whisper are highly accurate, but their predictions remain difficult to interpret. Existing explainable AI (XAI) methods often lack faithfulness and precise temporal grounding. We propose Listening with Entropy-guided Attention for Faithful explainability (LEAF-X), a model-intrinsic XAI framework for transformer-based ASR. LEAF-X combines entropy-guided attention weighting, multi-layer attention rollout, and optional causal ablations to identify low-entropy, high-impact heads and layers, producing sparse token-to-frame attributions. Unlike perturbation-based explainers or raw attention maps, LEAF-X exploits the internal structure of encoder-decoder and speech-augmented decoder-only models to generate explanations that better reflect model computation. Results show 32% improved faithfulness, 35-39% stronger locality/sparsity, and the most stable attributions, supporting more transparent and auditable ASR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LEAF-X, an explainability framework for transformer-based ASR models that improves faithfulness, locality, sparsity, and stability of predictions.
AI.motivation: Current transformer-based ASR models provide accurate results but lack interpretability, making it harder to audit and understand the predictions.
AI.method: LEAF-X employs entropy-guided attention weighting, multi-layer attention rollout, and optional causal ablations to produce token-to-frame attributions within ASR models intrinsically.
AI.result: LEAF-X improves prediction faithfulness by 32%, locality/sparsity by 35-39%, and offers the most stable attribution methods among existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: LEAF-X enhances the transparency and auditability of transformer-based ASR models, addressing gaps in faithful explainability and temporal grounding.

title: Recovery thresholds for hidden weighted sparse graphs
summary: Recovering structural information from noisy high-dimensional data is a fundamental task in statistical inference. We investigate the recovery thresholds for a graph hidden in a randomly weighted complete graph. Specifically, an unknown graph $H^* \in H_n$ is chosen uniformly at random, and hidden in a complete graph of $n$ vertices as follows: the weight of an edge $e \in H$ is distributed independently according to $P_n$; otherwise the weight is distributed independently according to $Q_n$. The goal is to recover almost all of $H$ from these edge weights. Assuming a local Lipschitzness of the Rényi divergence between distributions $P_n$ and $Q_n$, and a mild density condition for the graphs $H_n$, we give a unified characterization of the information-theoretic limit for recovering almost all of $H$ (also known as almost exact recovery). Our characterization connects the KL divergence between $P_n$ and $Q_n$ to the logarithm of the first moment threshold of $H$ in the Erdős-Rényi random graph model $G(n,p)$. Our lower bound also extends to the task of partial recovery, in which only a constant $λ$-fraction of $H$ needs to be recovered. Last but not least, for certain Bernoulli and Exponential regimes, and for Gaussian distributions, we are able to show an All-or-Nothing (AoN) threshold phenomenon at the exponential scale.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates recovery thresholds for graphs hidden in noisy data, providing a unified characterization of recovery limits based on statistical measures like KL divergence.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of extracting structural information from high-dimensional, noisy data, focusing on recovering graphs hidden within weighted networks under specific probabilistic models.
AI.method: The method involves characterizing recovery thresholds using information-theoretic measures, such as KL divergence and Rényi divergence, combined with conditions on graph density and edge weight distributions.
AI.result: The results establish a connection between the Rényi divergence and recovery probabilities, showing recovery limits for tasks like almost exact and partial recovery, along with an All-or-Nothing threshold phenomenon for specific distributions.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a theoretical framework for understanding recovery limits in noisy graph data, bridging statistical theory with practical inference scenarios involving graph structures.

title: Learning Coordinated Preference for Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-objective multi-agent reinforcement learning (MOMARL) models team decision making under multiple, potentially conflicting objectives. In this setting, conflicts arise not only across objectives but also across agents with different observations, roles, and contributions. We propose Preference Coordinated Multi-agent Policy Optimization (PCMA), which learns coordinated agent-specific preferences to enable complementary trade-offs among agents. Theoretically, we formulate cooperative MOMARL as a team-optimal game and show that, under suitable conditions, preference diversity can induce team improvement through a first-order improvement decomposition. Experiments on multiple cooperative MOMA environments and a practical traffic-control scenario show that PCMA improves both performance and trade-off coordination.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PCMA, a method to improve team coordination in cooperative multi-objective multi-agent scenarios.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the challenge of addressing conflicts in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning under multiple conflicting objectives among agents.
AI.method: The authors propose the PCMA framework, which learns agent-specific preferences for complementary trade-offs, supported by a theoretical formulation of the problem as a team-optimal game and validation through improvement decomposition.
AI.result: PCMA delivers enhanced performance and trade-off coordination in various cooperative multi-agent environments, including a traffic-control scenario.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the effectiveness of PCMA in resolving conflicts and improving team decision-making in cooperative multi-agent settings.

title: Machine-learned particle flow as a foundation model for collider physics
summary: The workflow from particle collision to physics analysis passes through a series of reconstruction steps that are traditionally modular and disconnected, with no shared representation linking low-level detector data to high-level analysis tasks. We show that casting event reconstruction as a machine learning problem naturally produces such a shared representation. We repurpose a machine learning model trained for particle-flow reconstruction (MLPF) to perform three distinct analysis tasks: jet flavor identification, jet energy regression, and missing momentum regression. By appending the per-particle latent representations learned during reconstruction as additional input features, we substantially improve over baselines that use kinematic features alone. We further demonstrate that a single linear layer trained using only the latent representations achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art baseline architectures, and outperforms the baseline for missing momentum regression with approximately 35 times fewer parameters. These results demonstrate that the latent representations learned during reconstruction encode essential physics information needed for downstream analysis, establishing MLPF as a foundation model and offering a concrete step toward an end-to-end pipeline from detector data to physics analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that machine learning (ML) for particle-flow reconstruction (MLPF) serves as an efficient and unified representation for both event reconstruction and downstream physics analysis tasks, outperforming traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditionally, event reconstruction and physics analysis tasks in particle physics are modularized without a shared representation, limiting efficiency and integration. The motivation is to unify these steps using ML to enhance performance and streamline the workflow.
AI.method: The authors repurpose MLPF, a machine learning model for particle-flow reconstruction, and append the latent representation it learns as input features for three downstream tasks: jet flavor identification, jet energy regression, and missing momentum regression. They compare its performance with kinematic baselines and state-of-the-art models.
AI.result: When using the latent representations learned during event reconstruction, performance significantly improves across tasks. A simple linear layer trained with these representations is competitive with state-of-the-art models while being substantially more parameter-efficient, especially excelling in missing momentum regression.
AI.conclusion: MLPF not only improves event reconstruction performance but also serves as a "foundation model" for downstream tasks. Its latent representations encode crucial physics information, enabling an efficient end-to-end pipeline from particle detector data to physics analysis.

title: Nonlinear Two-Time-Scale Stochastic Approximation: A Sharp Phase Transition and How to Beat It
summary: Recent finite-time analyses of nonlinear two-time-scale stochastic approximation show that under contractive assumptions the slow iterate $Y_k$ with stepsizes $β_k=Θ(k^{-1})$ and $α_k=Θ(k^{-a})$, $a\in(1/2,1)$, generally satisfies a mean-square rate of order $k^{-a}$; decoupled $k^{-1}$ rates require strong local linearity. We identify a sharp regularity-dependent boundary. In a rate-determining normal form where the slow drift contains a locally linear leakage and a nonlinear remainder of order $1+ρ$ ($ρ\in[0,1]$), the uncorrected recursion satisfies \[ \mathbb{E}\|Y_k\|^2 \le C\bigl(k^{-1}+k^{-a(1+ρ)}\bigr), \] and a matching scalar Gaussian lower bound shows that the slower term is unavoidable without modifying the update. Thus the decoupled $k^{-1}$ rate is guaranteed for the uncorrected recursion exactly when $a(1+ρ)\ge 1$. This lower bound concerns only the naive update; it is not an information-theoretic obstruction. We demonstrate this by equipping the normal-form recursion with an auxiliary online bias estimator \[ M_{k+1}=M_k+γ_k(R(X_k)-M_k),\qquad β_k\llγ_k\llα_k, \] and subtracting $M_k$ from the slow update. Under the same stability, moment, and remainder assumptions, the corrected recursion achieves $\mathbb{E}\|\widetilde Y_k\|^2=O(k^{-1})$ for every $ρ\in[0,1]$, including regimes where the uncorrected update provably suffers the slower rate. Finally, we prove localized transfer theorems that extend the phase-transition mechanism to general nonlinear TTSA in fast-manifold coordinates. The proofs are non-asymptotic and rely on two Abel-transform cancellations: one for the locally linear fast-error leakage, and one for the tracked nonlinear bias.

AI.tldr: This research analyzes two-time-scale stochastic approximations under contractive assumptions, showcasing that the slow iteration rate depends on specific regularity conditions, with corrections leading to guaranteed improvements in convergence rate.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the regularity-dependent limitations in rates of convergence for two-time-scale stochastic approximation, identifying scenarios where faster rates are achievable through updates.
AI.method: The authors analyze rate-determining forms with nonlinear leakage and propose an auxiliary online bias estimator to correct the slow update mechanism, demonstrating its impact through mathematical proofs.
AI.result: Uncorrected updates achieve slower rates as predicted by mean-square analysis, while the corrected recursion employing the online bias estimator consistently reaches optimal rates under diverse conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed auxiliary bias correction eliminates slower rate limitations in two-time-scale stochastic approximation, proving its effectiveness beyond the uncorrected methods. It extends rate improvement mechanisms to broader nonlinear systems.

title: CANN-EUCLID: unsupervised constitutive artificial neural network model discovery from full-field data
summary: Constitutive artificial neural networks (CANNs) provide interpretable material model discovery, but have so far been used in stress-supervised settings based on apparent stress-strain data from homogeneous tests. Because each test samples only a narrow loading path and provides homogenized rather than local stress information, robust discovery typically requires multiple loading modes to constrain the multidimensional response. This is challenging for soft biological tissues, where repeated testing, damage, and sample variability limit reliable information from a single specimen. Here, we combine CANNs with the stress-unsupervised full-field discovery framework EUCLID to identify sparse hyperelastic laws directly from displacement fields and reaction forces in one heterogeneity-inducing loading case. CANN-EUCLID minimizes equilibrium imbalance with sparsity-promoting regularization selecting compact active terms, without local stress measurements or a prescribed law. We evaluate the approach on isotropic and anisotropic benchmarks with prescribed ground-truth laws. When the ground truth is representable by the chosen CANN basis, our method recovers the correct terms with near-exact accuracy, including exponential terms with embedded parameters. When it is not contained in the basis, the method retains shared terms and approximates missing contributions using available basis functions. Generalization depends strongly on sampled deformation states: exponential strain-stiffening terms can be recovered accurately when sufficiently probed, but can produce large extrapolation errors when the stiffening regime lies outside the sampled domain. Forward FE validation simulations show that the discovered behavior accurately replicates the ground truth. These results establish stress-unsupervised CANN discovery as a promising framework for interpretable full-field constitutive model identification.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a stress-unsupervised method combining Constitutive Artificial Neural Networks (CANNs) and a discovery framework called EUCLID to derive material models directly from displacement fields and reaction forces.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by challenges in robust material model discovery for soft biological tissues, which are difficult to test repeatedly due to damage, variability, and limited reliable data from single specimens.
AI.method: The method integrates CANNs with EUCLID to minimize equilibrium imbalance and apply sparsity-promoting regularization. It derives constitutive laws from a single heterogeneous loading scenario without requiring local stress measurements or predefined laws.
AI.result: The approach successfully identifies ground-truth laws when the truth is representable within the CANN basis and approximates when not. Results generalize well when deformation states are adequately sampled and are validated through forward finite element simulations.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that stress-unsupervised CANN discovery is a valuable framework for interpretable and effective full-field material model identification.
